questions.prompt: What is the primary goal of Pairwise (All-Pairs) Testing?
options: To test every possible combination of inputs.
To guarantee that every pair of parameter values is tested together at least once.
To test only the default or happy-path configurations.
To randomly guess which combinations will fail.
questions.explanation: Pairwise testing ensures that all possible pairs of parameter values are covered, drastically reducing test cases while maintaining high coverage of interaction bugs.

questions.prompt: Why is exhaustive testing (testing all combinations) usually impossible in real-world systems?
options: Because users don't care about bugs in rare combinations.
Because modern computers are too slow.
Due to the combinatorial explosion, the number of test cases grows exponentially with each new parameter.
Because developers write perfect code for complex combinations.
questions.explanation: Adding just a few parameters multiplies the required test cases exponentially, quickly reaching thousands or millions of scenarios.

questions.prompt: Research indicates that the vast majority of software defects are caused by:
options: A single parameter or the interaction of exactly two parameters.
The interaction of three or more specific parameters.
Hardware failures rather than software logic.
questions.explanation: Most bugs are single-mode or double-mode faults. This is the foundational theory that makes Pairwise testing so effective.
